what_it_does: Renders a styled, single-page HTML board that visually inventories seven architectural layers of an application—from surface UI and pilot modes through commander, cognition, models, memory, and the addressed substrate—using cards, status badges, and a unification thesis.
observations: Self-contained HTML with embedded CSS and no JavaScript or external dependencies.
Uses CSS custom properties for a dark, responsive theme and a responsive card grid.
Divides content into seven numbered layers, each with a colored left accent and a header.
Each card lists a title, a primary primitive/URI, and a short 'serves' description.
Uses 'live' and 'building / to build' badges to indicate implementation status.
States a thesis: the user is the pilot, the RHM is the engine, and everything is a projection from one substrate.
References concrete primitives such as /api/territory, dials(op=set), channel_act, run_role, and resolve_address.
Ends with a footer that restates the unification in one line and a legend for status badges.
standouts: The 'one address space / one substrate' framing that ties all layers together.
The pilot/autopilot/dials metaphor as a unifying interaction model.
The file functions as both a design document and a visual presentation.
conventions: Follows semantic HTML with header, sections, and footer.
Uses CSS custom properties for theming.
Uses BEM-ish class names such as layer, lhead, card, and ctitle.
Uses inline style overrides for per-layer accent colors.
Breaks typical code-file conventions by being a static conceptual artifact rather than executable logic.
concerns: Content is hardcoded, not generated from the substrate it describes.
Status claims like 'live' and 'building' cannot be verified from the file alone.
Metaphor-heavy language may obscure precise technical meaning.
No navigation, search, or interactive filtering for a large conceptual board.
notes: A dark-themed, seven-layer HTML board that inventories existing and in-flight architectural organs under a pilot-and-substrate unification narrative.
questions: What is the exact relationship between this HTML board and the actual substrate or address space it describes?
Which items marked 'building' are already implemented versus aspirational?
Is this file meant to be served dynamically, or is it static documentation?
Is the intended audience human readers, the RHM, or both?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to put 'everything on the table' as a unified, projectable ledger of code/design substrates; the file does exactly that as a static visual board, though it describes a dynamic system it does not itself implement.
apparent_themes: Unification through a single addressable typed substrate.
Human pilot agency with adjustable autopilot dials.
Composability and projection instead of rebuilding.
Live inventory of capabilities with explicit status.
intention_signals: It is trying to communicate that the application's many organs are already built and only need to be composed, projected, and piloted from a single surface, using a consistent pilot/autopilot interaction model.
novelty: The framing of an entire application as a 'space' flown by a pilot, where UI, RHM, memory, models, and substrate are all projections from one typed address space, presented as a visual board with live/building status badges.
contribution: Provides a human-readable, layered map of the system's conceptual architecture and capability inventory, including explicit status labels and a unifying narrative that a fused system can use as documentation, onboarding material, or a reference for what belongs in the one address space.
summary_for_embedding: A dark-themed HTML board that enumerates seven layers of an application architecture—surface, modes, commander, cognition, models, memory, and substrate—as cards with live/building status badges under a unifying pilot-and-substrate thesis.
intention_for_embedding: To present a single-page visual ledger showing that the system's organs already exist and should be composed, projected, and piloted rather than rebuilt.
